fullDescription.text: The application redirects users to a URL supplied in a request parameter without validating that the target URL belongs to a trusted domain. An attacker can craft a link that redirects victims to a phishing or malicious site while the URL appears to originate from the trusted application.

shortDescription.text: No scheme restriction on redirect target allows javascript: and data: URIs

fullDescription.text: The redirect endpoint performs no parsing of the URL scheme. Besides redirecting to external HTTPS domains, an attacker may supply javascript: or data: scheme URIs. While Flask's redirect() issues a 302 with a Location header (so javascript: URIs do not execute directly), some intermediate proxies and older browsers may interpret these schemes differently, and data: URIs can serve attacker-controlled HTML content.

message.text: GET /api/redirect accepts an unvalidated 'next' query parameter and passes it directly to Flask's redirect(). No scheme, host, or allowlist check is performed, so an attacker can redirect users to any external domain (e.g. /api/redirect?next=https://evil.example.com) enabling phishing attacks. The application imports urlparse but never uses it in this handler.

description.text: Validate that the redirect target is a relative path or belongs to an allowlisted domain before redirecting. Parse the URL with urlparse and reject any scheme/netloc that is not in a trusted set.

message.text: The redirect endpoint accepts arbitrary URI schemes because no URL parsing or allowlist is applied to the 'next' parameter. An attacker could supply next=//evil.com (protocol-relative URL) to bypass naive checks that only look for 'http' prefixes, or next=data:text/html,<script>... to serve malicious content. The urlparse module is imported but unused in this handler.

description.text: Flask API blueprint with multiple vulnerability patterns including open redirect at /api/redirect
